text: Supplementation with 2000 mg calcium and 800 IU vitamin D per day reduces stress fracture incidence by approximately 20% in female military recruits during basic training.
specific_value: 20% reduction with 2000 mg calcium + 800 IU vitamin D/day

text: Stress fractures occur at a rate of approximately 5.9% per 8 weeks in female military recruits undergoing basic training without supplementation.
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation is a cost-effective population-level intervention for preventing stress fractures in physically active females undergoing rapid increases in training load.
specific_value: ~187 fractures prevented per 14,416 women over 24 months

text: Stress fractures are among the most common and debilitating overuse injuries in populations undergoing sudden high-volume training, affecting both military and athletic populations.
population: general population undergoing high training loads

text: The protective effect of calcium and vitamin D supplementation against stress fractures is consistent in both intention-to-treat (20% reduction) and per-protocol (21% reduction) analyses, indicating a robust effect.
